insights: Cost Reduction goal durations in the Finance department increased more than twofold from January (171.3 days) to December (371.2 days) 2022, confirming a severe and monotonic deterioration in goal execution speed.

Owner assignment is the dominant driver of duration variation, explaining 59.8% of total variance—far exceeding all other dimensions—indicating that who owns the goal matters more than goal type, state, or priority.

Two interlocking mechanisms drive the trend: slower-performing owners were systematically assigned goals later in the year (composition effect, r = 0.996), and the same 30 multi-goal owners consistently degraded in performance over time (within-owner degradation, r = 0.989).

Even after controlling for owner identity, a strong residual monthly increase in duration persists (r = 0.958), confirming that both composition shift and individual performance decline independently and substantially contribute to the overall slowdown.
Individual owner mean durations span a wide range (165 to 382 days), revealing high operational heterogeneity—suggesting inconsistent capability, support, or accountability across goal owners.

The near-perfect correlations between timing and both owner slowness and personal degradation imply systemic issues in assignment logic and performance sustainability—not isolated incidents or random fluctuations.
summary: The sharp 117% increase in Cost Reduction goal durations across the Finance department in 2022 is driven primarily by owner-related factors—not goal design or prioritization. Both structural misalignment (slower owners receiving later assignments) and functional erosion (all repeat owners worsening over time) are statistically dominant and mutually reinforcing causes. This reflects a breakdown in either goal assignment governance, owner capacity planning, or ongoing performance support—rather than isolated underperformance. The extreme concentration of variance in the 'owner' dimension (60%) signals that interventions must target ownership practices first: reviewing assignment criteria, diagnosing root causes of individual slowdowns, and implementing timely coaching or workload rebalancing. Next focus should be on validating whether recent owner assignments follow documented criteria and identifying the top 3–5 owners contributing most to the trend for targeted operational review.
